text: Da Vinci single site cholecystectomy unedited console time. Many people believe that robotic surgery adds significant time to the otherwise efficient procedure of laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Here we'll show the unedited console time demonstrating that the time for surgical dissection of the gallbladder and its removal are similar to that for a standard laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

The surgeon begins dissection of Calot's triangle using a Maryland Grasper and Dissector in the right arm of the robotic system, while a Cobra Grasper in the left arm holds the base of the gallbladder. A bedside assistant uses a grasper to retract the gallbladder fundus and liver superiorly.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection of the cystic duct using a Maryland dissector, with stable visualization provided by the laparoscopic camera. The single site instruments demonstrate mobility and flexibility, crossing when necessary to facilitate dissection maneuvers.

The single-site robotic instruments are visible, demonstrating mobility and proficiency in grasping and retracting tissues. Despite lacking articulation or wrist action, the instruments efficiently create windows around anatomical structures.

The surgeon continues the dissection using single-site instruments, which are positioned similarly to those in a standard laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The graspers and dissectors manipulate the tissues effectively, despite the lack of wrist articulation found in multi-site robotic procedures.

The surgeon clips the fatty lymphatic tissues dissected from the anterior surface of the cystic artery using Hem-o-lok clips to ensure hemostasis. The visual field is withdrawn for a wider view with the robotic platform, allowing the surgeon to control the camera and instruments. The clips are inspected for proper positioning and locked, followed by the use of a cautery hook to divide the tissues, ensuring hemostasis without clipping the gallbladder side.

The surgeon uses a hook cautery device to perform blunt dissection around the cystic duct lymph node, aiming to identify the cystic artery. The cautery aids in achieving hemostasis during the dissection.
Calot's triangle is clearly delineated, and windows have been created behind the cystic duct and artery. Hemlock clips are applied to secure both the cystic duct and the cystic artery.

The surgeon clips the cystic artery using Hemlock clips, ensuring secure ligation. Two clips are placed on the cystic duct for internal retention, and one clip is used to ligate the base of the gallbladder. The cystic duct is divided using scissors due to the close proximity of the clips. The surgeon then switches to a hook device to divide the cystic artery, controlling back bleeding with cautery.

The surgeon uses a hook cautery device to continue dissecting the gallbladder from the liver bed, employing a combination of blunt and cautery dissection to define the plane between the gallbladder and liver. The semi-rigid single site robotic instruments pass through curving metal trochars, becoming less rigid as more of the instrument is exposed. This results in instrument whip, causing the cautery to occasionally contact the liver, which is monitored closely to avoid damage to delicate structures like the hepatic duct, common duct, or duodenum.

The surgeon manipulates the gallbladder to expose its posterior wall for dissection, utilizing the flexibility of single-site robotic instruments. The high-definition robotic camera aids in delineating the plane between the liver and gallbladder. The gallbladder is nearly completely dissected from the liver, allowing inspection of the liver bed for hemostasis, with cauterization applied to a few areas. The clips are inspected and found secure, leading to the amputation of the gallbladder from the liver, completing the dissection.
The assistant uses a grasper to hold the base of the gallbladder, and the gallbladder, instruments, trocars, and single site port are removed through the umbilical incision as a single unit.
